name: Barilla MIO · IAAD Admission Test Concept

content.value: This is the prototype of a messaging application based on car license plates. Users can register using their car's license plate to verify ownership. To communicate with other users, simply frame or manually enter the car's license plate and see if the user has an account.
But... Why would it be useful to use license plates to communicate?

This idea came about one morning when I noticed a car parked with an open door, but I was passing by and wanted to notify the owner somehow. This gave rise to the problem of not being able to communicate, having only the license plate and model of the car. Actually, it is a chat between “cars,” where users are identified not by their name, but by the model of the car.
The application should allow replacing traditional tickets left on the dashboard, taking advantage of the technological devices that we all now possess.

I developed the first wireframes and prototypes of the application, however, I could not proceed with the full development due to my limited computer skills. The implementation of such an application requires advanced knowledge in security protocols and messaging, as well as the implementation of license plate recognition algorithms.
Ealt is a new concept in minimalist health apps. The concept is for Ealt to be the first app you open as soon as you pick up your phone in the morning, not social, email or games.

The idea is to bring into one app the main applications that a user can and does use to get ready for the day, from the clothes to put on to the physical activity Ealt provides:

- Location
- Date (day, month, year)
- Temperature
- Humidity
- Weather situation (sunny, rainy, cloudy, snow)
- Today Tips: Small phrases that provide advice based on weather situations
- Hours of sleep (yet to be confirmed)
- Steps
- Heart rate
- Height
- Weight

name: iPadOS calculator Concept
description: Concept for a native iPadOS calculator: a swipe-accessible popup calculator designed for quick, seamless use without leaving the current app.

content.value: This is a concept illustrating what the iPadOS native calculator might look like, an idea often mentioned by Apple's senior vice president Craig Federighi. Currently, iPads do not have a native “calculator” app as they do on iOS, as there are many quality alternatives on the market and Apple has not yet developed a single app that meets the requirements for iPadOS.

Instead of devising a full-screen version, I thought of a popup version. This would make the calculator more accessible and easy to use while using other apps. In fact, we often need a calculator while we are already engaged in another activity on the iPad, and having to interrupt that activity to open a separate app is inconvenient.

My prototype works similarly to other iPadOS features, emerging from the corners such as the “quick note” feature. Swiping with a finger or the Apple Pencil from the outside to the inside of the screen would bring up the calculator quickly and intuitively.

This is a personally made prototype of an extremely compact indoor security camera. Current solutions available on the market are often relatively bulky, despite the fact that there is no real need for space for electronics. While analyzing a Xiaomi model, I noticed a waste of interior space.
I therefore decided to design a camera with more discreetly camouflaged shapes for the home environment.
Due to its size, it can be easily placed on shelves, bedside tables and furniture without being obtrusive. The design was created using Figma, without the aid of 3D modeling software.
